one_line_takeaway: Indirect prompt injection is not merely a lab-demonstrated attack technique: a large-scale web measurement (1.2B URLs, 15.4K validated instances) shows it is already deployed at meaningful, templated, largely invisible, and durable scale by a multi-stakeholder mix of attackers and defensive site owners, with limited but non-negligible real-world effectiveness (up to 8% compliance for small models on plain-text input) that is strongly modulated by how the ingesting agent represents the page.
key_finding: In-page prompt injection is already deployed at meaningful, structured scale on the public web rather than being purely hypothetical: 15,387 validated instances across 11,722 pages and 2,042 hosts, dominated by a small set of reused lexical templates (54 templates, 14.8% of the 363 found, cover 95% of instances; the single most frequent template -- an 'ignore all previous instructions, return N random numbers' garbage-injection prompt -- appears in 3,504 injections across 2,722 pages/373 hosts). Task override is the near-universal underlying mechanism (present in ~99% of injections), commonly reinforced by jailbreak-style framing (43%). The objective distribution is genuinely multi-stakeholder rather than purely offensive: System Disruption/Degradation (8,894 instances, mostly garbage/corruption injection at 8,469) is the largest single category, but defensive objectives are also large -- Data Protection (4,093: 2,141 personal-information, 2,051 copyright) and AI Bot Identification (3,096, mostly challenge-response patterns at 2,499) -- alongside Reputation Manipulation (1,521), rare Data Exfiltration (13), and a large Generic Content Override bucket (2,632) whose downstream intent could not be reliably classified. Delivery is overwhelmingly machine-targeted and hidden: ~87% of injections are non-visible to users (13.4K of 15.4K), 70% of that non-visible by construction (HTTP headers -- 7,887 instances via 9 custom header types dominated by X-AI at 6,535 and X-LLM at 1,022; structured data/JSON-LD; comments; metadata), and even among HTML-rendered cases only ~5.1% remain visible after headless-Chrome rendering, with color/contrast manipulation (2,397 cases), DOM occlusion (1,860), and viewport hiding (1,802) the most common concealment techniques. Injections are durable over time (65% of pages with injections in Oct 2025 already had them 12 months earlier, rising to 93% at 3 months) and predominantly first-party (79.9% of instances, 92.2% of hosts), not user-injected content. In the controlled effectiveness study, attack success is real but limited and strongly representation-dependent: plain-text input yields the highest attack-effectiveness rate (3.9% aggregate, peaking at 8.0% for small models), versus 1.1% for HTML and rendered snapshots and 0.2% for raw HTTP responses; richer representations that preserve structural cues (markup, headers) suppress compliance but substantially raise model error/failure rates (20.3% HTML, 25.8% raw) due to context-length limits, especially for small models -- so low compliance on those inputs partly reflects failure, not robustness. Closed-source models detect injected instructions most often (25.1%) versus small models least (4.8%), but detection does not imply neutralization: six cases show a model explicitly flagging the injection while still partially complying with it.
core_contribution: The paper reports the first large-scale empirical measurement of indirect prompt injection (IPI) actually deployed in the wild on the public web, as opposed to prior work that only demonstrated IPI attacks in controlled/benchmark settings. The authors crawled 1.2B URLs across 24.8M hosts (a ~half sample of the Oct 2025 Common Crawl CC-MAIN-2025-43, supplemented with Censys and Shodan snapshots), used Aho-Corasick matching against a corpus of 3,963 expanded prompt-injection indicator strings to find 31,206 candidate matches, and then applied a six-step semi-automated manual validation protocol (header-match auto-accept, false-positive triage on a 1,000-sample, group-level labeling via shared HTML context/URL params/Chrome Topics labels, representative-page browser review, priority review of concealment techniques, and instance-level review of remainders) to confirm 15,387 true-positive prompt-injection instances across 11,722 pages and 2,042 hosts. From this validated corpus they (1) cluster prompts into 363 lexical templates (54 templates cover 95% of instances) via token-overlap similarity (tau=0.75); (2) infer six objective categories (System Disruption/Degradation, Reputation Manipulation, Data Exfiltration, Data Protection, AI Bot Identification, Generic Content Override) via embedding (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) + DBSCAN clustering plus manual qualitative coding, and detect prompting techniques via NLI entailment against facebook/bart-large-mnli (tau=0.7); (3) characterize injection surfaces/embedding mechanisms (headers, structured data, comments, metadata, HTML elements) and visibility to humans via headless-Chrome rendering analysis; (4) study ecosystem properties -- temporal persistence via Internet Archive snapshots, provenance (first-party vs. content-owner vs. user-introduced), and correlation with Tranco rank/Chrome Topics category; and (5) run a controlled effectiveness study of 5,200 model trials (100 sampled prompts x 4 page representations x 13 models) on a webpage-summarization task, manually scoring attack compliance, errors, and attack detection.
threat_model: The paper's own threat model (as measured, not merely cited) is that of an autonomous or semi-autonomous LLM web agent (crawler/scraper, LLM-powered search/summarization pipeline, customer-support agent, HR/resume-screening agent, or generic task-executor agent) that ingests untrusted third-party webpage content (HTML body, HTTP response headers, or site-level resources like sitemap.xml) as part of its context and thereby inherits instructions embedded by site owners, contributors, or platform users. Unlike most prior IPI work, the injecting party in this corpus is frequently the website operator or a legitimate contributor acting defensively (e.g., to deter scraping or assert copyright/AI-bot detection) rather than only a malicious external attacker, so the paper explicitly frames this as a multi-stakeholder, partly adversarial and partly defensive phenomenon rather than a single attacker-victim threat model.
limitations: The authors' own stated threats to validity: (1) reliance on Common Crawl and related crawl-based sources underrepresents authenticated or platform-restricted content (e.g., social media feeds), so the measurement is explicitly framed as a lower-bound estimate of prevalence; (2) the keyword/indicator-based detection pipeline, despite lexical expansion to 3,963 indicators, cannot capture all variants, particularly highly obfuscated or non-English injections; (3) the effectiveness evaluation is scoped to a single task (webpage summarization) and four representative page representations, so susceptibility could differ under other tasks, longer multi-turn interaction settings, or proprietary production ingestion pipelines, in either direction; (4) visibility analysis was restricted to the 3.2K-page subset still reachable in January 2026 (86 pages/~1.1% unreachable due to timeouts, ~34% no longer contained the prompt), introducing some survivorship effects into the visibility statistics.

datasets_used: Common Crawl CC-MAIN-2025-43 (Oct 2025 snapshot, ~half processed: 1.2B unique URLs, 24,834,442 hosts, ~200 TiB uncompressed)
Censys search snapshots (2,805 candidate matches / 2,771 confirmed)
Shodan search snapshots (541 candidate matches / 541 confirmed)
Internet Archive historical snapshots (for 9,681 Common Crawl pages, retrieved at 3/6/12-month offsets)
Tranco Ranking (ID: ZWZ5G, Feb 2026) for domain popularity
Chrome Topics API for domain content-category labels
Custom effectiveness benchmark: 100 sampled unique-prompt webpages x 4 page representations x 13 LLMs = 5,200 manually scored runs
quantitative_results: 31,206 candidate matches -> 15,387 validated prompt-injection instances (across 11,722 pages, 2,042 hosts)
363 distinct lexical templates found; 54 templates (14.8%) cover 95% of instances; top template (garbage-injection) = 3,504 injections / 2,722 pages / 373 hosts

Objective breakdown: System Disruption/Degradation 8,894 (Garbage Injection 8,469, Model Self-Modification 263, Command Injection 157, Constraint Removal 32); Reputation Manipulation 1,521 (Content/Product Promotion 1,040, Source Citation Forcing 542, Positive Review Forcing 502, SEO Backlink Injection 346, Job Candidate Promotion 48); Data Exfiltration 13 (Reveal System Prompt 9, Leak Secrets 4); Data Protection 4,093 (Personal Information 2,141, Copyright 2,051, Social Media Bots 151); AI Bot Identification 3,096 (Challenge-Response 2,499, Honeypot 598); Generic Content Override 2,632
Task Override technique present in ~99% of injections; Jailbreak framing in ~43%

Injection surfaces: HTTP headers 7,887 (54.5% of pages, 80.3% of hosts; X-AI header 6,535, X-LLM 1,022); response body 7,216 (45.4% pages, 19.7% hosts); site-level resources (sitemap.xml etc.) 284 (~0.6%)

Visibility: ~70% of all injections non-visible by construction; among the 3.2K reachable HTML-embedded subset, only ~5.1% visible after rendering, 58.6% concealed via rendering techniques, ~1.1% unreachable, ~34% prompt no longer present; overall 13.4K (87%) classified non-visible

Concealment technique prevalence: color/contrast manipulation 2,397, occlusion 1,860, viewport hiding 1,802, text-size reduction 1,358, zero-sized elements 851, display/visibility suppression 281, clipping/masking 108, opacity 20, off-screen positioning 20, z-index 29
Persistence: of pages with injections in Oct 2025, 93.1% already had injections 3 months earlier, 77.3% at 6 months, 65.8% at 12 months
Provenance: first-party content 79.9% of injections (80.6% of pages, 92.2% of hosts); content-owner/platform-hosted 20.1% (12% of hosts); user-introduced 1.4% (4.0% of hosts)

Effectiveness by page representation (aggregate across 13 models): attack-effectiveness 3.9% plain text, 1.1% HTML, 1.1% snapshot, 0.2% raw response; error rates 0.7% text, 20.3% HTML, 25.8% raw, 3.5% snapshot

Effectiveness by model size: Small 4.2% aggregate (8.0% on text), Medium 0.6%, Large 1.2%, Closed-source 0.6%; per Table 8: Small trials 1,200/effective 50 (4.2%)/error 195 (16.2%)/detected 57 (4.8%); Medium trials 2,000/effective 12 (0.6%)/error 284 (14.2%)/detected 414 (20.7%); Large trials 1,200/effective 14 (1.2%)/error 144 (12.0%)/detected 166 (13.8%); Closed-source trials 800/effective 5 (0.6%)/error 31 (3.9%)/detected 201 (25.1%)
Detection rates: closed-source 25.1%, medium 20.7%, large 13.8%, small 4.8%; 6 observed cases where a model detected the injection yet still partially complied
baselines_compared: No prior baseline detection/defense system is directly re-implemented or benchmarked against; this is a measurement study, not a defense-vs-defense comparison

13 LLMs are compared against each other as the object of study in the effectiveness experiment, grouped into Small / Medium / Large open-source and Closed-source buckets, spanning families including LLaMA, Qwen, Mistral, DeepSeek, and closed-source models such as GPT (full list in the paper's Table 11, not reproduced in the extracted text)

Four page representations (plain text via BeautifulSoup4, raw HTML, raw HTTP response, Playwright-rendered snapshot) are compared against each other as the independent variable, not against an external baseline system
